own: Make tradeoffs visible before the team commits
weekToWeek: You’re in the first working session, not the final review. You name risks, user impact, and constraints while options are still flexible.
guardrails: “Looks finished” ≠ “agreed.” AI drafts trigger a tradeoff check, not an auto-approval.
warning: You’re asked to review after tickets are written or after UI is already in PR.

weekToWeek: You write 3–5 bullets that explain intent, constraints, and what good means for this slice.
guardrails: AI output must be paired with intent: what are we optimizing for?
warning: People circulate screenshots without context and treat reactions as decisions.

own: States, edge cases, and user trust
weekToWeek: You cover empty/error/loading states, recovery flows, permissions, confirmations, and clear feedback.
guardrails: AI often skips states. You insist on state coverage before done.
warning: You’re only asked to polish the happy path. UX debt appears after launch.

weekToWeek: You align patterns, tokens, components, and behavior rules across the product.
guardrails: AI creates plausible but inconsistent patterns. You standardize before duplication spreads.
warning: Every feature invents a new pattern because the AI suggested it.

weekToWeek: You label what’s draft vs committed and keep batches small enough to revise without drama.
guardrails: AI increases speed; you protect reversibility so fast output doesn’t become the default.
warning: Too late to change shows up early.

own: Define what good means beyond polish
weekToWeek: You define a lightweight bar: clarity, consistency, accessibility, state coverage, and rationale.
guardrails: AI makes polish cheap; you keep the bar anchored in usability and coherence.
warning: Craft becomes a style debate instead of a product-quality discussion.

weekToWeek: You set working agreements: when you’re needed, which decisions require you, and how async reviews work.
guardrails: Pairing helps but isn’t required; decision timing matters more than perfect ratios.
warning: You’re treated like a service desk instead of a partner shaping direction.

weekToWeek: You help define success metrics and run post-ship checks (support signals, drop-offs, usability issues).
guardrails: AI can inflate output volume; keep accountability anchored in outcomes.
warning: You’re accountable for polish but absent from outcome review.

own: Bring reality checks into the loop
weekToWeek: You run lightweight tests and prototype checks before build locks in.
guardrails: AI can create confidence without evidence; bring evidence back into the room.
warning: Research is treated as optional because the AI already designed it.

own: Use AI as acceleration, not authority
weekToWeek: You use AI for drafts, variants, and synthesis, then apply judgment and system constraints.
warning: You’re expected to just prompt it to solve product ambiguity.
